INN Hotels Project¶

Context¶

A significant number of hotel bookings are called-off due to cancellations or no-shows. The typical reasons for cancellations include change of plans, scheduling conflicts, etc. This is often made easier by the option to do so free of charge or preferably at a low cost which is beneficial to hotel guests but it is a less desirable and possibly revenue-diminishing factor for hotels to deal with. Such losses are particularly high on last-minute cancellations.

The new technologies involving online booking channels have dramatically changed customers’ booking possibilities and behavior. This adds a further dimension to the challenge of how hotels handle cancellations, which are no longer limited to traditional booking and guest characteristics.

The cancellation of bookings impact a hotel on various fronts:

  • Loss of resources (revenue) when the hotel cannot resell the room.
  • Additional costs of distribution channels by increasing commissions or paying for publicity to help sell these rooms.
  • Lowering prices last minute, so the hotel can resell a room, resulting in reducing the profit margin.
  • Human resources to make arrangements for the guests.

Objective¶

The increasing number of cancellations calls for a Machine Learning based solution that can help in predicting which booking is likely to be canceled. INN Hotels Group has a chain of hotels in Portugal, they are facing problems with the high number of booking cancellations and have reached out to your firm for data-driven solutions. You as a data scientist have to analyze the data provided to find which factors have a high influence on booking cancellations, build a predictive model that can predict which booking is going to be canceled in advance, and help in formulating profitable policies for cancellations and refunds.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of customers' booking details. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary

  • Booking_ID: unique identifier of each booking
  • no_of_adults: Number of adults
  • no_of_children: Number of Children
  • no_of_weekend_nights: Number of weekend nights (Saturday or Sunday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel
  • no_of_week_nights: Number of week nights (Monday to Friday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel
  • type_of_meal_plan: Type of meal plan booked by the customer:
    • Not Selected – No meal plan selected
    • Meal Plan 1 – Breakfast
    • Meal Plan 2 – Half board (breakfast and one other meal)
    • Meal Plan 3 – Full board (breakfast, lunch, and dinner)
  • required_car_parking_space: Does the customer require a car parking space? (0 - No, 1- Yes)
  • room_type_reserved: Type of room reserved by the customer. The values are ciphered (encoded) by INN Hotels.
  • lead_time: Number of days between the date of booking and the arrival date
  • arrival_year: Year of arrival date
  • arrival_month: Month of arrival date
  • arrival_date: Date of the month
  • market_segment_type: Market segment designation.
  • repeated_guest: Is the customer a repeated guest? (0 - No, 1- Yes)
  • no_of_previous_cancellations: Number of previous bookings that were canceled by the customer prior to the current booking
  • no_of_previous_bookings_not_canceled: Number of previous bookings not canceled by the customer prior to the current booking
  • avg_price_per_room: Average price per day of the reservation; prices of the rooms are dynamic. (in euros)
  • no_of_special_requests: Total number of special requests made by the customer (e.g. high floor, view from the room, etc)
  • booking_status: Flag indicating if the booking was canceled or not.

Importing necessary libraries and data¶

In [164]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# for visualizing data
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# For randomized data splitting
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, GridSearchCV, StratifiedKFold # Import GridSearchCV and StratifiedKFold

# Removing the limit for displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
# Setting the limit for displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 200)


# For building models for prediction
import statsmodels.stats.api as sms
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.tools.tools import add_constant
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier # Import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn import tree # Import the tree module for plotting


# Calling different metric scores
from sklearn.metrics import (
    f1_score,
    accuracy_score,
    recall_score,
    precision_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    roc_auc_score,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
    precision_recall_curve,
    roc_curve,
    make_scorer # Import make_scorer
)

required_car_parking_space 36275.0 0.030986 0.173281 0.0 0.0 0.00 0.0 1.0
lead_time 36275.0 85.232557 85.930817 0.0 17.0 57.00 126.0 443.0
arrival_year 36275.0 2017.820427 0.383836 2017.0 2018.0 2018.00 2018.0 2018.0
arrival_month 36275.0 7.423653 3.069894 1.0 5.0 8.00 10.0 12.0
arrival_date 36275.0 15.596995 8.740447 1.0 8.0 16.00 23.0 31.0
repeated_guest 36275.0 0.025637 0.158053 0.0 0.0 0.00 0.0 1.0
no_of_previous_cancellations 36275.0 0.023349 0.368331 0.0 0.0 0.00 0.0 13.0
no_of_previous_bookings_not_canceled 36275.0 0.153411 1.754171 0.0 0.0 0.00 0.0 58.0
avg_price_per_room 36275.0 103.423539 35.089424 0.0 80.3 99.45 120.0 540.0
no_of_special_requests 36275.0 0.619655 0.786236 0.0 0.0 0.00 1.0 5.0
  • no_of_adults:75% of bookings have at least 2 adults.
  • no_of_children:There is a huge difference between the 75th percentile and the max indicating outliers. 75% of the observations are 0.
  • no_of_weekend_nights:The average number of weekend nights is about 1. There is also a big difference between the 75th percentile and max indicating outliers in this column.
  • no_of_week_nights: The average number of weeknights is ~2. There is a large difference between the 75th percentile and max indicating outliers or possibly a mistake.
  • required_car_parking_space: 75% of observations are 0, indicating most guests don't require parking space.
  • lead_time:The average lead time is ~85 days. 75% of bookings have 126 days lead time with the max at 443 indicating outliers.
  • The arrival year with the most bookings is close to 2018. The min is 2017 and the max is 2018, so the data just focuses on these two years.
  • 75% of the bookings occur in October.
  • 75% of the bookings are on the 23rd of the month, with the average on the 15th.
  • no_of_previous_cancellations:The average of number of previous cancellations rounds down to 0 at 0.02. There is a large gap between the 75th percentile and max indicating outliers. 75% of observations are 0.
  • no_of_previous_bookings_not_cancelled: There is a large difference between the 75th percentile and max indicating outliers. Also, 75% of observations are 0.
  • avg_price_per_room:The average price per room is ~103 euros. There is a large gap between the 75th percentile and max indicating outliers.
  • no_of_special_requests: 75% of guests have had 1 special request. The max is 5 and at least 50% of guests have none.

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Leading Questions¶

1. What are the busiest months in the hotel?

In [14]:
# Creating a column for month names for readability
df['month_name'] = pd.to_datetime(df['arrival_month'], format='%m').dt.strftime('%b')

month_name

  • The busiest months are October(5,317 bookings or 14.6% of bookings) followed by September(4,611 bookings or 12.7%) and August(3,813 bookings or 10.5%).
  • The slowest months are January(1,014 bookings or 2.8%) followed by February with 1,704 bookings(4.7%) and March with 2,358 bookings(6.5%).

2. Which market segment do most of the guests come from?

  • Most of the guests(at 63.9%) come from the Online market segment.
  • The least popular market segment type is Aviation at 0.3%.

3. Hotel rates are dynamic and change according to demand and customer demographics. What are the differences in room prices in different market segments?

In [19]:
# Filtering out the 'Complementary' market segment type
df_filtered = df[df['market_segment_type'] != 'Complementary']

# Seting the figure size
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

# Using the 'Spectral' color palette
palette = sns.color_palette("Spectral", n_colors=df['market_segment_type'].nunique())

# Creating the boxplot
sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered, x='market_segment_type', y='avg_price_per_room', palette=palette)

# Add red horizontal lines at y=90 and y=110
plt.axhline(y=90, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
plt.axhline(y=110, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)

# Customize labels and title
plt.xlabel('Market Segment Type', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Average Room Price', fontsize=12)
plt.title('Average Room Price by Market Segment Type', fontsize=14)

# Rotate x labels for readability if needed
plt.xticks(rotation=30)

plt.show()

/tmp/ipython-input-2320850444.py:11: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered, x='market_segment_type', y='avg_price_per_room', palette=palette)
/tmp/ipython-input-2320850444.py:11: UserWarning: The palette list has more values (5) than needed (4), which may not be intended.
  sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered, x='market_segment_type', y='avg_price_per_room', palette=palette)
  • Online market segment types consistently book the most expensive rooms on average with 50% of bookings costing 90 to 130 euros.
  • The Corporate market segment types consistently book the least expensive rooms with 50% of bookings costing 70 to 95 euros.

4. What percentage of bookings are canceled?

In [20]:
# Getting percentage of bookings canceled
cancel_rate = (df['booking_status'].eq('Canceled').mean()) * 100
print(f"Percentage of bookings canceled: {cancel_rate:.2f}%")

Percentage of bookings canceled: 32.76%
  • 32.76% of bookings are canceled.

5. Repeating guests are the guests who stay in the hotel often and are important to brand equity. What percentage of repeating guests cancel?

In [21]:
# Filtering only repeating guests
repeat_guests = df[df['repeated_guest'] == 1]

# Calculating how many of them canceled
num_canceled = repeat_guests[repeat_guests['booking_status'] == 'Canceled'].shape[0]

# Calculating total number of repeat guests
total_repeat = repeat_guests.shape[0]

# Finding percentage
percentage_canceled = (num_canceled / total_repeat) * 100

print(percentage_canceled)

1.7204301075268817
  • 1.7% of repeating guests canceled their booking.

6. Many guests have special requirements when booking a hotel room. Do these requirements affect booking cancellation?

In [22]:
"""df2 will convert Canceled to 1 and Not_Canceled to 0"""

# Creating a copy to manipulate
df2 = df.copy()

# Converting Canceled/Not_Canceled into 1 and 0 and putting in column 'Canceled'
df2['Canceled'] = df2['booking_status'].map({'Canceled': 1, 'Not_Canceled': 0})


# Creating a correlation matrix
corr = df2.corr(numeric_only=True)

# Plot heatmap with diverging color scale
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(
    corr,
    cmap='coolwarm',   # blue = negative, red = positive
    annot=True,
    fmt='.2f',
    linewidths=0.5,
    center=0            # ensures 0 correlation is white/neutral
)

plt.title('Correlation Heatmap', fontsize=14)
plt.show()
  • no_of_special_requests is negatively correlated to canceled bookings suggesting guests with higher numbers of special requests are less likely to cancel.
  • It also looks like lead_time has a moderately positive correlation with Canceled. This suggests greater lead times increase the chances of canceled bookings.
  • avg_price_per_room is somewhat positively correlated with no_of_children and no_of_adults.

Univariate Analysis¶

In [23]:
def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
    kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=df, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data=df, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        df2[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram
In [24]:
# Calling boxplot and histogram of avg_price_per_room
histogram_boxplot(df2, 'avg_price_per_room')

Observations

  • The avg_price_per_room is right skewed with an outlier close to 530 euros.
  • Some of the rooms are priced way above others with the median at about 100 euros.
In [25]:
# Calling boxplot and histogram of lead_time
histogram_boxplot(df2, 'lead_time')

Observations

  • The mean lead time is about 65 to 70 days and the median is about 90 days.
  • The plot is right skewed with many outliers greater than or at about 300 days.
  • 50% of the bookings occur with a lead time of 20 to 120 days.
In [26]:
# Function to create labeled barplots
def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
    """
    Barplot with percentage at the top

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
    n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all levels)
    """

    total = len(data[feature])  # length of the column
    count = data[feature].nunique()
    if n is None:
        plt.figure(figsize=(count + 2, 6))
    else:
        plt.figure(figsize=(n + 2, 6))

    plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
    ax = sns.countplot(
        data=data,
        x=feature,
        palette="Paired",
        order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n],
    )

    for p in ax.patches:
        if perc == True:
            label = "{:.1f}%".format(
                100 * p.get_height() / total
            )  # percentage of each class of the category
        else:
            label = p.get_height()  # count of each level of the category

        x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2  # width of the plot
        y = p.get_height()  # height of the plot

        ax.annotate(
            label,
            (x, y),
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            size=12,
            xytext=(0, 5),
            textcoords="offset points",
        )  # annotate the percentage

    plt.show()  # show the plot

Observations on Number of Adults¶

In [39]:
# Calling barplot with percentage
labeled_barplot(df, "no_of_adults", perc=True)

  ax = sns.countplot(
  • 72% of bookings are for 2 adults.
  • There are very few observations with 4 adults.

Observations on Type of Meal Plan¶

In [30]:
# Calling barplot with percentage
labeled_barplot(df, "type_of_meal_plan", perc=True)

/tmp/ipython-input-500166299.py:20: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  ax = sns.countplot(
  • At about 76%, most guests chose the 1st Meal Plan which only included breakfast.
  • The majority of the rest did not opt for a meal plan or chose Meal Plan 2 which included Breakfast and 1 other meal.

Observations on Type of Room Reserved¶

In [31]:
# Calling barplot with percentage
labeled_barplot(df, "room_type_reserved", perc=True)

/tmp/ipython-input-500166299.py:20: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  ax = sns.countplot(
  • The most popular room type by far was Room Type 1.
  • Very few customers booked Room Types 5, 7 and 3.

Observations on Arrival Year¶

In [32]:
# Calling barplot with percentage
labeled_barplot(df, "arrival_year", perc=True)

/tmp/ipython-input-500166299.py:20: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  ax = sns.countplot(
  • 82% of the bookings are from 2018. It's likely the hotel has garnered more business overall since 2017.

Bivariate Analysis¶

In [33]:
# Calling heatmap of all numeric variables
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 7))
sns.heatmap(df2.corr(numeric_only = True), annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral")
plt.show()
  • Repeated guest and number of previous bookings not canceled have a positive correlation with each other which is expected.
  • Number of previous cancellations and number of previous bookings not canceled also have a positive correlation.
  • Average price per room is positively correlated with number of adults and number of children which is expected.
  • Lead time is postively correlated to number of bookings canceled.
  • Number of special requests is somewhat negatively correlated to canceled status.
In [34]:
# Function to plot distributions with respect to target


def distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, predictor, target):

    fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))

    target_uniq = data[target].unique()

    axs[0, 0].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[0]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[0]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 0],
        color="teal",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[0, 1].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[1]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[1]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 1],
        color="orange",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[1, 0].set_title("Boxplot w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=target, y=predictor, ax=axs[1, 0], palette="gist_rainbow")

    axs[1, 1].set_title("Boxplot (without outliers) w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data,
        x=target,
        y=predictor,
        ax=axs[1, 1],
        showfliers=False,
        palette="gist_rainbow",
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Canceled vs Lead Time¶

  • The boxplots indicate bookings with higher lead times are more likely to cancel.

  • The third quartile on both sets of boxplots indicate that the more expensive rooms are more likely to cancel.
  • There are outliers in both distributions, especially above the ~200 euros mark.
In [63]:
# Function to plot stacked bar chart
def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
    """
    Print the category counts and plot a stacked bar chart

    data: dataframe
    predictor: independent variable
    target: target variable
    """
    count = data[predictor].nunique() # Use the provided data DataFrame
    # Use df2 to get the sorting order based on 'Canceled'
    sorter = df2['Canceled'].value_counts().index[-1]
    tab1 = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], margins=True).sort_values( # Use the provided data DataFrame
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    print(tab1)
    print("-" * 120)
    tab = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], normalize="index").sort_values( # Use the provided data DataFrame
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    tab.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, figsize=(count + 5, 6))
    plt.legend(
        loc="lower left", frameon=False,
    )
    plt.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1))
    plt.show()

Market Segment Type vs Canceled¶

In [46]:

market_segment_type                     
All                  24390  11885  36275
Online               14739   8475  23214

Corporate             1797    220   2017

  • The plot shows that guests who booked online are more likely to cancel than any other market segment type.
  • Offline and Aviation market segments have about the same rate of cancellation.
  • Corporate and complimentary have the least percentage of cancellations.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  • This plot shows that guests that ordered Meal Plan 2 were most likely to cancel.
  • Not selected and Meal Plan 1 had about the same percentage of cancellations.
  • Meal plan 3 had the least amount of cancellations.

numerical_col.remove("repeated_guest")
numerical_col.remove("required_car_parking_space")

    plt.boxplot(df2[variable], whis=1.5)

Preparing data for modeling

In [110]:
# Identifying the independent and dependent variables
X = df3.drop(["Canceled"], axis=1)
Y = df3["Canceled"]

# adding a constant to the independent variables
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# creating dummy variables
X = pd.get_dummies(X, drop_first=True)
X = X.astype(float)

# splitting data in train and test sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, Y, test_size=0.30, random_state=1
)
In [111]:
# Printing training/test set stats
print("Shape of Training set : ", X_train.shape)
print("Shape of test set : ", X_test.shape)
print("Percentage of classes in training set:")
print(y_train.value_counts(normalize=True))
print("Percentage of classes in test set:")
print(y_test.value_counts(normalize=True))

Shape of test set :  (10883, 22)

1    0.329356

Percentage of classes in test set:
Canceled
0    0.676376
1    0.323624
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

Checking Multicollinearity¶

  • In order to make statistical inferences from a logistic regression model, it is important to ensure that there is no multicollinearity present in the data.
In [112]:
# Creating variance inflation factor series
vif_series = pd.Series(
    [variance_inflation_factor(X_train.values, i) for i in range(X_train.shape[1])],
    index=X_train.columns,
    dtype=float,
)
print("Series before feature selection: \n\n{}\n".format(vif_series))

Series before feature selection:

arrival_date                           1.006114
avg_price_per_room                     1.884585
no_of_special_requests                 1.225517
type_of_meal_plan_Meal Plan 2          1.202719
type_of_meal_plan_Meal Plan 3          1.024471
type_of_meal_plan_Not Selected         1.231913

dtype: float64
  • Some types of market segments display high multicollinearity.
  • We will drop market_segment_type_Online and market_segment_type_Offline one at a time and test model performance.

Dropping market_segment_type_Online

    [variance_inflation_factor(X_train2.values, i) for i in range(X_train1.shape[1])],
    index=X_train1.columns,
)
print("Series before feature selection: \n\n{}\n".format(vif_series2))

Building a Logistic Regression model¶

In [125]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using statsmodels
def model_performance_classification_statsmodels(
    model, predictors, target, threshold=0.5
):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    threshold: threshold for classifying the observation as class 1
    """

    # checking which probabilities are greater than threshold
    pred_temp = model.predict(predictors) > threshold
    # rounding off the above values to get classes
    pred = np.round(pred_temp)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf
In [126]:
# Fitting logistic regression model
logit2 = sm.Logit(y_train, X_train2.astype(float))
lg2 = logit2.fit(disp=False)

print("Training performance:")
model_performance_classification_statsmodels(lg2, X_train2, y_train)

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/statsmodels/discrete/discrete_model.py:2385: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in exp
  return 1/(1+np.exp(-X))
/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/statsmodels/discrete/discrete_model.py:2443: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in log
  return np.sum(np.log(self.cdf(q * linpred)))

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
LinAlgError                               Traceback (most recent call last)
/tmp/ipython-input-2409634152.py in <cell line: 0>()
      1 logit2 = sm.Logit(y_train, X_train2.astype(float))
----> 2 lg2 = logit2.fit(disp=False)
      3 
      4 print("Training performance:")
      5 model_performance_classification_statsmodels(lg2, X_train2, y_train)

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/statsmodels/discrete/discrete_model.py in fit(self, start_params, method, maxiter, full_output, disp, callback, **kwargs)
   2599     def fit(self, start_params=None, method='newton', maxiter=35,
   2600             full_output=1, disp=1, callback=None, **kwargs):
-> 2601         bnryfit = super().fit(start_params=start_params,
   2602                               method=method,
   2603                               maxiter=maxiter,

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/statsmodels/discrete/discrete_model.py in fit(self, start_params, method, maxiter, full_output, disp, callback, **kwargs)
    241             pass  # TODO: make a function factory to have multiple call-backs
    242 
--> 243         mlefit = super().fit(start_params=start_params,
    244                              method=method,
    245                              maxiter=maxiter,

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/statsmodels/base/model.py in fit(self, start_params, method, maxiter, full_output, disp, fargs, callback, retall, skip_hessian, **kwargs)
    580             Hinv = cov_params_func(self, xopt, retvals)
    581         elif method == 'newton' and full_output:
--> 582             Hinv = np.linalg.inv(-retvals['Hessian']) / nobs
    583         elif not skip_hessian:
    584             H = -1 * self.hessian(xopt)

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/numpy/linalg/_linalg.py in inv(a)
    606     with errstate(call=_raise_linalgerror_singular, invalid='call',
    607                   over='ignore', divide='ignore', under='ignore'):
--> 608         ainv = _umath_linalg.inv(a, signature=signature)
    609     return wrap(ainv.astype(result_t, copy=False))
    610 

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/numpy/linalg/_linalg.py in _raise_linalgerror_singular(err, flag)
    102 
    103 def _raise_linalgerror_singular(err, flag):
--> 104     raise LinAlgError("Singular matrix")
    105 
    106 def _raise_linalgerror_nonposdef(err, flag):

LinAlgError: Singular matrix
  • We are getting the singular matrix error which could indicate that there are still multicollinear variables present.
In [127]:
# Checking for constant or duplicate columns
(X_train2.nunique() == 1).sum()
X_train2.loc[:, X_train2.nunique() == 1].head()
Out[127]:
const
13662 1.0
26641 1.0
17835 1.0
21485 1.0
5670 1.0
In [128]:
# Checking for perfect separation
for col in X_train2.columns:
    unique_vals = y_train.groupby(X_train2[col]).nunique()
    if unique_vals.min() == 1:
        print(f"Perfect separation detected in column: {col}")

Perfect separation detected in column: no_of_week_nights
Perfect separation detected in column: lead_time
Perfect separation detected in column: avg_price_per_room
Perfect separation detected in column: no_of_special_requests
Perfect separation detected in column: market_segment_type_Complementary
  • Since market_segment_type_Complementary is the only categorical variable and therefore the only variable capable of perfect separation, this is worth looking into.
In [129]:
# Looking at how many cancellations Complementary has
df3.groupby('market_segment_type_Complementary')['Canceled'].value_counts()
Out[129]:
count
market_segment_type_Complementary Canceled
False 0 23999
1 11885
True 0 391

  • Market segment type Complementary has 0 cancellations and therefore is creating perfect separation which causes a singular matrix.

Removing market_segment_type_Complementary

In [130]:
# Dropping Complementary market segment type
X_train3 = X_train.drop("market_segment_type_Complementary", axis=1)
vif_series3 = pd.Series(
    [variance_inflation_factor(X_train3.values, i) for i in range(X_train3.shape[1])],
    index=X_train3.columns,
)
print("Series before feature selection: \n\n{}\n".format(vif_series2))

Series before feature selection:

In [133]:
# Fitting the logistic regression function
logit3 = sm.Logit(y_train, X_train3.astype(float))
lg3 = logit3.fit(disp=False)

Model performance evaluation¶

In [134]:
# Calling model performance from statsmodels
print("Training performance:")
model_performance_classification_statsmodels(lg3, X_train3, y_train)

Training performance:
Out[134]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.800016 0.617721 0.733078 0.670474
  • The logistic regression training model achieved an accuracy of 80% with a recall of 61%, a precision of 73% and F1 score at 0.67. The results indicate the model works reasonably well at predicting cancellations with a good balance between identifying true cancellations and minimizing false positives.

Final Model Summary¶

In [136]:
# Naming and running test model
X_test3 = X_test[X_train3.columns]

# Predicting test results
y_pred_prob = lg3.predict(X_test3)
y_pred = (y_pred_prob >= 0.5).astype(int)
In [138]:
# Importing ROC Curve
from sklearn.metrics import RocCurveDisplay

# ROC Curve
RocCurveDisplay.from_predictions(y_test, y_pred_prob)
plt.title("ROC Curve - Logistic Regression (Test Set)")
plt.show()

# Confusion Matrix Heatmap
sns.heatmap(confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred), annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='coolwarm')
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Logistic Regression (Test Set)")
plt.show()
In [139]:
# Calling functions from sklearn.metrics to compute scores
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report

print("Test Performance:")
print("Accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("Precision:", precision_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("Recall:", recall_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("F1:", f1_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("ROC-AUC:", roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_prob))
print("\nConfusion Matrix:\n", confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred))

Test Performance:
Accuracy: 0.80097399614077
Precision: 0.725249169435216
Recall: 0.6198182850653038
F1: 0.6684017146356399
ROC-AUC: 0.8600012682522441

Confusion Matrix:
 [[6534  827]
 [1339 2183]]

*The logistic regression model showed a strong predictive performance on the test set with an AUC of 0.86 and overall accuracy of 80%. The model correctly identified 62% of actual cancellations while maintaining a precision of 73%. This indicates the predictions are reliable with relatively few false positives. The results suggest the model can effectively support the hotel in predicting booking cancellations reducing revenue loss.

Building a Decision Tree model¶

In [142]:
# Building decision tree model
model = DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion="gini", random_state=1)
model.fit(X_train3, y_train)
Out[142]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
In [143]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using sklearn
def model_performance_classification_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf
In [144]:
# defining a function to create confusion matrix to check performance
def confusion_matrix_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """
    To plot the confusion_matrix with percentages

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """
    y_pred = model.predict(predictors)
    cm = confusion_matrix(target, y_pred)
    labels = np.asarray(
        [
            ["{0:0.0f}".format(item) + "\n{0:.2%}".format(item / cm.flatten().sum())]
            for item in cm.flatten()
        ]
    ).reshape(2, 2)

    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=labels, fmt="")
    plt.ylabel("True label")
    plt.xlabel("Predicted label")
In [145]:
# Checking performance on training set
decision_tree_perf_train = model_performance_classification_sklearn(
    model, X_train3, y_train
)
decision_tree_perf_train
Out[145]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.994014 0.98601 0.995773 0.990868
In [146]:
# Creating confusion matrix
confusion_matrix_sklearn(model, X_train3, y_train)

decision_tree_perf_test = model_performance_classification_sklearn(
    model, X_test3, y_test
)
decision_tree_perf_test

Accuracy Recall Precision F1

In [173]:
# Creating list of column names from X_train3
feature_names = list(X_train3.columns)
print(feature_names)

['const', 'no_of_adults', 'no_of_weekend_nights', 'no_of_week_nights', 'lead_time', 'arrival_month', 'arrival_date', 'avg_price_per_room', 'no_of_special_requests', 'type_of_meal_plan_Meal Plan 2', 'type_of_meal_plan_Meal Plan 3', 'type_of_meal_plan_Not Selected', 'room_type_reserved_Room_Type 2', 'room_type_reserved_Room_Type 3', 'room_type_reserved_Room_Type 4', 'room_type_reserved_Room_Type 5', 'room_type_reserved_Room_Type 6', 'room_type_reserved_Room_Type 7', 'market_segment_type_Corporate', 'market_segment_type_Offline', 'market_segment_type_Online']
In [154]:
# Creating decision tree visual
plt.figure(figsize=(20, 30))

out = tree.plot_tree(
    model,
    feature_names=feature_names,
    filled=True,
    fontsize=9,
    node_ids=True,
    class_names=True,
)
for o in out:
    arrow = o.arrow_patch
    if arrow is not None:
        arrow.set_edgecolor("black")
        arrow.set_linewidth(1)
plt.show()

Do we need to prune the tree?¶

  • The decison tree classified almost every training booking perfectly. While seemingly ideal, this is too perfect for real world data and indicates overfitting. The model simply memorized the training data without learning generalized patterns. Without pruning or limitation, this would lower predictive accuracy.
In [161]:
# Calling model feature importances
importances = model.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="teal", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()
  • Lead time, average price per room and offline market segment type were the 3 most important features to focus on.
In [167]:
# Guards to reduce overfitting
base_params = dict(min_samples_leaf=10, min_samples_split=20, random_state=1)

# Getting pruning path
big_tree = DecisionTreeClassifier(**base_params)  # max_depth=None
big_tree.fit(X_train3, y_train)
alphas = big_tree.cost_complexity_pruning_path(X_train3, y_train).ccp_alphas

# subsample alphas
alphas = np.unique(np.quantile(alphas, np.linspace(0, 1, 20)))

# CV pick best alpha using F1
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=1)
scores = []
for a in alphas:
    clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(ccp_alpha=a, **base_params)
    s = cross_val_score(clf, X_train3, y_train, cv=cv, scoring='f1', n_jobs=-1)
    scores.append(s.mean())

best_alpha = alphas[int(np.argmax(scores))]
pruned_tree = DecisionTreeClassifier(ccp_alpha=best_alpha, **base_params).fit(X_train3, y_train)

# evaluate on test
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score
y_pred  = pruned_tree.predict(X_test3)
y_prob  = pruned_tree.predict_proba(X_test3)[:,1]

print("Test Accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("Test Precision:", precision_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("Test Recall:", recall_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("Test F1:", f1_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("Test ROC-AUC:", roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob))

Test Accuracy: 0.8705320224202885
Test Precision: 0.815090963316433
Test Recall: 0.7759795570698467
Test F1: 0.7950545454545455
Test ROC-AUC: 0.9232675955920058
In [184]:
# Visualizing pruned tree
model_to_plot = pruned_tree

plt.figure(figsize=(18, 10))
tree.plot_tree(
    model_to_plot,
    feature_names=X_test3.columns,
    class_names=['Not Canceled', 'Canceled'],
    filled=True,
    rounded=True,
    fontsize=9,
    proportion=True,
    max_depth=3
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [185]:
print(tree.export_text(pruned_tree, feature_names=feature_names, show_weights=True))

Model Performance Comparison and Conclusions¶

In [186]:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

# -------------------------------------------------
# 0) Fit models (skip/modify if yours are already fit)
# -------------------------------------------------
# Original (unpruned) tree
try:
    orig_tree
except NameError:
    orig_tree = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)  # vanilla tree
    orig_tree.fit(X_train3, y_train)

# pruned_tree should already be trained from your pruning step


# -------------------------------------------------
# 1) Helper to compute metrics
# -------------------------------------------------
def eval_clf(clf, X, y):
    y_pred = clf.predict(X)
    return pd.Series({
        "Accuracy":  accuracy_score(y, y_pred),
        "Recall":    recall_score(y, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "Precision": precision_score(y, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "F1":        f1_score(y, y_pred, zero_division=0),
    })

def compare_models(models, names, X, y, round_to=4):
    out = pd.concat([eval_clf(m, X, y) for m in models], axis=1)
    out.columns = names
    return out.T.round(round_to)


# -------------------------------------------------
# 2) TRAIN set comparison
# -------------------------------------------------
models = [orig_tree, pruned_tree]
names  = ["Decision Tree (Original)", "Decision Tree (Post-Pruned)"]

models_train_comp_df = compare_models(models, names, X_train3, y_train)
print("Train set performance comparison:")
display(models_train_comp_df)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 3) TEST set comparison
# -------------------------------------------------
models_test_comp_df = compare_models(models, names, X_test3, y_test)
print("Test set performance comparison:")
display(models_test_comp_df)

Train set performance comparison:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Decision Tree (Original) 0.9940 0.986 0.9958 0.9909
Decision Tree (Post-Pruned) 0.8727 0.782 0.8226 0.8018 
Test set performance comparison:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Decision Tree (Original) 0.8659 0.8012 0.7880 0.7946
Decision Tree (Post-Pruned) 0.8705 0.7760 0.8151 0.7951
  • Training Set: The original decision tree demonstrated extremely high performance (Accuracy ≈ 0.994, Recall ≈ 0.986, Precision ≈ 0.996, F1 ≈ 0.991). These numbers were too perfect, indicating overfitting the training data. The post-pruned tree shows lower scores (Accuracy ≈ 0.872, Recall ≈ 0.782, Precision ≈ 0.823, F1 ≈ 0.802), which are more realistic and expected after pruning.
  • Test Set: The original tree declined sharply in performance on testing data (Accuracy ≈ 0.866, Recall ≈ 0.801, Precision ≈ 0.788, F1 ≈ 0.795), confirming that overfitting compromised accurate predictions. The post-pruned tree mildly improved overall predictions (Accuracy ≈ 0.871, Recall ≈ 0.776, Precision ≈ 0.815, F1 ≈ 0.795). These balanced metrics indicate more stability and interpretability while maintaining comparable predictive power.
  • Conclusion: Pruning reduced model complexity and overfitting without too much accuracy loss. The post-pruned tree generalizes better and is the preferred model for predicting hotel booking cancellations.

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

What profitable policies for cancellations and refunds can the hotel adopt?

  • Installing cancellation fees or asking for higher deposits for bookings with greater lead times (higher risk)
  • For customers predicted more likely to cancel, they could offer a non-refundable discount rate to retain revenue
  • For moderate to low risk customers, the hotel could offer credit based rebooking instead of full refunds

What other recommedations would you suggest to the hotel?

  • The hotel could send out confirmation emails to high risk customers which would reduce accidental cancellations
  • They could also offer loyalty points or perks to repeated customers to encourage retention

from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

from google.colab import files

notebook_name = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Content/ARodriguez_Project_SLC_DSBA_INNHotels_FullCode.ipynb"
html_output_name = notebook_name.replace('.ipynb', '.html')

get_ipython().system(f'jupyter nbconvert --to html "{notebook_name}"')
files.download(html_output_name)

[NbConvertApp] WARNING | pattern '/content/drive/MyDrive/Content/ARodriguez_Project_SLC_DSBA_INNHotels_FullCode.ipynb' matched no files
This application is used to convert notebook files (*.ipynb)
        to various other formats.

        WARNING: THE COMMANDLINE INTERFACE MAY CHANGE IN FUTURE RELEASES.

Options
=======
The options below are convenience aliases to configurable class-options,
as listed in the "Equivalent to" description-line of the aliases.
To see all configurable class-options for some <cmd>, use:
    <cmd> --help-all

--debug
    set log level to logging.DEBUG (maximize logging output)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level=10]
--show-config
    Show the application's configuration (human-readable format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config=True]
--show-config-json
    Show the application's configuration (json format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config_json=True]
--generate-config
    generate default config file
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.generate_config=True]
-y
    Answer yes to any questions instead of prompting.
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.answer_yes=True]
--execute
    Execute the notebook prior to export.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.enabled=True]
--allow-errors
    Continue notebook execution even if one of the cells throws an error and include the error message in the cell output (the default behaviour is to abort conversion). This flag is only relevant if '--execute' was specified, too.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.allow_errors=True]
--stdin
    read a single notebook file from stdin. Write the resulting notebook with default basename 'notebook.*'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.from_stdin=True]
--stdout
    Write notebook output to stdout instead of files.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class=StdoutWriter]
--inplace
    Run nbconvert in place, overwriting the existing notebook (only
            relevant when converting to notebook format)
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory=]
--clear-output
    Clear output of current file and save in place,
            overwriting the existing notebook.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --ClearOutputPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--coalesce-streams
    Coalesce consecutive stdout and stderr outputs into one stream (within each cell).
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --CoalesceStreamsPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--no-prompt
    Exclude input and output prompts from converted document.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True]
--no-input
    Exclude input cells and output prompts from converted document.
            This mode is ideal for generating code-free reports.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True]
--allow-chromium-download
    Whether to allow downloading chromium if no suitable version is found on the system.
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.allow_chromium_download=True]
--disable-chromium-sandbox
    Disable chromium security sandbox when converting to PDF..
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.disable_sandbox=True]
--show-input
    Shows code input. This flag is only useful for dejavu users.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input=False]
--embed-images
    Embed the images as base64 dataurls in the output. This flag is only useful for the HTML/WebPDF/Slides exports.
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.embed_images=True]
--sanitize-html
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized..
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html=True]
--log-level=<Enum>
    Set the log level by value or name.
    Choices: any of [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 'DEBUG', 'INFO', 'WARN', 'ERROR', 'CRITICAL']
    Default: 30
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level]
--config=<Unicode>
    Full path of a config file.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.config_file]
--to=<Unicode>
    The export format to be used, either one of the built-in formats
            ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf']
            or a dotted object name that represents the import path for an
            ``Exporter`` class
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.export_format]
--template=<Unicode>
    Name of the template to use
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_name]
--template-file=<Unicode>
    Name of the template file to use
    Default: None
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_file]
--theme=<Unicode>
    Template specific theme(e.g. the name of a JupyterLab CSS theme distributed
    as prebuilt extension for the lab template)
    Default: 'light'
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.theme]
--sanitize_html=<Bool>
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized.This
    should be set to True by nbviewer or similar tools.
    Default: False
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html]
--writer=<DottedObjectName>
    Writer class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: 'FilesWriter'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class]
--post=<DottedOrNone>
    PostProcessor class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.postprocessor_class]
--output=<Unicode>
    Overwrite base name use for output files.
                Supports pattern replacements '{notebook_name}'.
    Default: '{notebook_name}'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.output_base]
--output-dir=<Unicode>
    Directory to write output(s) to. Defaults
                                  to output to the directory of each notebook. To recover
                                  previous default behaviour (outputting to the current
                                  working directory) use . as the flag value.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--FilesWriter.build_directory]
--reveal-prefix=<Unicode>
    The URL prefix for reveal.js (version 3.x).
            This defaults to the reveal CDN, but can be any url pointing to a copy
            of reveal.js.
            For speaker notes to work, this must be a relative path to a local
            copy of reveal.js: e.g., "reveal.js".
            If a relative path is given, it must be a subdirectory of the
            current directory (from which the server is run).
            See the usage documentation
            (https://nbconvert.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#reveal-js-html-slideshow)
            for more details.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--SlidesExporter.reveal_url_prefix]
--nbformat=<Enum>
    The nbformat version to write.
            Use this to downgrade notebooks.
    Choices: any of [1, 2, 3, 4]
    Default: 4
    Equivalent to: [--NotebookExporter.nbformat_version]

Examples
--------

    The simplest way to use nbconvert is

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to html

            Options include ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf'].

            > jupyter nbconvert --to latex mynotebook.ipynb

            Both HTML and LaTeX support multiple output templates. LaTeX includes
            'base', 'article' and 'report'.  HTML includes 'basic', 'lab' and
            'classic'. You can specify the flavor of the format used.

            > jupyter nbconvert --to html --template lab mynotebook.ipynb

            You can also pipe the output to stdout, rather than a file

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --stdout

            PDF is generated via latex

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to pdf

            You can get (and serve) a Reveal.js-powered slideshow

            > jupyter nbconvert myslides.ipynb --to slides --post serve

            Multiple notebooks can be given at the command line in a couple of
            different ways:

            > jupyter nbconvert notebook*.ipynb
            > jupyter nbconvert notebook1.ipynb notebook2.ipynb

            or you can specify the notebooks list in a config file, containing::

                c.NbConvertApp.notebooks = ["my_notebook.ipynb"]

            > jupyter nbconvert --config mycfg.py

To see all available configurables, use `--help-all`.
